audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man phoning a woman who lives in an English city called Banford to get some advice about moving to that city.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Linda speaking.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, Linda.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: This is Matt Brooks.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Alex White gave me your number.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: He said you'd be able to give me some advice about moving to Banford.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Yes, Alex did mention you.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: How can I help?
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Well, uh, first of all, which area to live in?
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Well, I live in Dalton, which is a really nice suburb, not too expensive, and there's a nice park.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Recommends a suburb called Dalton.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Dalton has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Linda speaking.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, Linda.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: This is Matt Brooks.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Alex White gave me your number.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: He said you'd be able to give me some advice about moving to Banford.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Yes, Alex did mention you.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: How can I help?
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Well, uh, first of all, which area to live in?
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Well, I live in Dalton, which is a really nice suburb, not too expensive, and there's a nice park.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Hmm. Do you know how much it would be to rent a 2 bedroom flat there?
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Yeah, you should be able to get something reasonable for £850 per month.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: That's what people typically pay.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: You certainly wouldn't want to pay more than £900 that doesn't include bills or anything.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: No, that sounds all right.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: I'll definitely have a look there.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Are the transport links easy from where you live?
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Uh. Well, I'm very lucky.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: I work in the city center, so I don't have to use public transport.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: I go by bike.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Oh, I wish I could do that.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Is it safe to cycle around the city?
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Yes, it's fine, and it keeps me fit.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Anyway, driving to work in the city center would be a nightmare, because there's hardly any parking.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: And the traffic during the rush hour can be bad.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: I'd be working from home, but I'd have to go to London one or two days a week.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Oh, that's perfect.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Getting to London is no problem.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: There's a fast train every 30 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Which only takes 45 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Yeah, the train service isn't bad during the week.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: And they run quite late at night.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: It's weekends that are a problem.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: They're always doing engineering work.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: And you have to take a bus to Hadham and pick up the train there, which is really slow.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: But other than that Banford's a great place to live.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: I've never been happier.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: There are some nice restaurants in the city center, and a brand new cinema, which has only been open a couple of months.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: There's a good arts center too.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Sounds like Banford's got it all.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Yes, we're really lucky.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: There are lots of really good aspects to living here.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: The schools are good, and the hospital here is one of the best in the country.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Everyone I know who's been there's had a positive experience.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Oh, I can give you the name of my dentist too, in Bridge Street, if you're interested.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: I've been going to him for years, and I've never had any problems.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Oh, OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: I'll find his number and send it to you.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Thanks, that would be really helpful.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Are you planning to visit Banford soon?
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Yes, my wife and I are both coming next week.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: We want to make some appointments with estate agents.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: I could meet you if you like, and show you around.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Are you sure?
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: We'd really appreciate that.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Either a Tuesday or Thursday is good for me, after 5:30.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Thursday is preferable - Tuesday, I need to get home before 6 pm.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: OK, great.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Let me know which train you're catching, and I'll meet you in the cafe outside.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: You can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: It's opposite the station, and next to the museum.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: Brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: I'll text you next week, then.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: Thanks so much for all the advice.
audioscript.speaker: MATT

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: I'll see you next week.
audioscript.speaker: LINDA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.items: Linda recommends living in suburb of: <input=Dalton>

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a fitness instructor talking on the radio about different ways of keeping fit.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, if you are one of those people who hasn't found the perfect physical activity yet, here are some things to think about, which might help you make the right decision for you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first question to ask yourself is whether you would enjoy training in a gym.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many people are put off by the idea of having to fit a visit to the gym into their busy day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You often have to go very early or late, as some gyms can get very crowded.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But with regular training, you'll see a big difference in a relatively short space of time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Running has become incredibly popular in recent years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's probably got a lot to do with the fact that it's a very accessible form of exercise.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Anyone can run, even if you could only run a few meters to begin with, but make sure you get the right shoes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's worth investing in a high quality pair, and they don't come cheap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another great thing about running is that you can do it at any time of day or night.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The only thing that may stop you is snow and ice.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Swimming is another really good way to build fitness.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What attracts many people is that you can swim in an indoor pool at any time of year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On the other hand, it can be quite boring or solitary.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's hard to chat to people while you're swimming lengths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Cycling has become almost as popular as running in recent years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's probably because as well as improving their fitness, many people say being out in the fresh air in a park or in the countryside can be fun.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Provided the conditions are right of course, only fanatics go out in the wind and rain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Yoga is a good choice for those of you looking for exercise, which focuses on developing both a healthy mind and body.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a good way of building strength.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And with the right instructor, there's less chance of hurting yourself than with other more active sports.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But don't expect to find it easy, it can be surprisingly challenging.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Especially for people who aren't very flexible.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Getting a personal trainer is a good way to start your fitness program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Obviously, there can be significant costs involved, but if you've got someone there to encourage you and help you achieve your goals, you're less likely to give up.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Make sure you get someone with a recognized qualification though, or you could do yourself permanent damage.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the radio program, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Whatever you do, don't join a gym, unless you're sure you'll make good use of it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So many people waste lots of money by signing up for membership, and then hardly ever go.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What happens to their good intentions?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I don't think people suddenly stop caring about improving their fitness, or decide they have more important things to do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I think people lose interest when they don't think they're making enough progress.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's when they give up hope, and stop believing they'll ever achieve their goals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Also, what people sometimes don't realize when they start is that it takes a lot of determination and hard work to keep training week after week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And lots of people don't have that kind of commitment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One thing you can do to help yourself is to set manageable goals, be realistic, and don't push yourself too far.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some people advise writing goals down, but I think it's better to have a flexible approach.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Give yourself a really nice treat every time you reach one of your goals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And don't get too upset if you experience setbacks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a journey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are bound to be difficulties along the way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Whatever you do, don't join a gym, unless you're sure you'll make good use of it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So many people waste lots of money by signing up for membership, and then hardly ever go.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What happens to their good intentions?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I don't think people suddenly stop caring about improving their fitness, or decide they have more important things to do.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I think people lose interest when they don't think they're making enough progress.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's when they give up hope, and stop believing they'll ever achieve their goals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Also, what people sometimes don't realize when they start is that it takes a lot of determination and hard work to keep training week after week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And lots of people don't have that kind of commitment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One thing you can do to help yourself is to set manageable goals, be realistic, and don't push yourself too far.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some people advise writing goals down, but I think it's better to have a flexible approach.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Give yourself a really nice treat every time you reach one of your goals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And don't get too upset if you experience setbacks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's a journey.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are bound to be difficulties along the way.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What advantage does the speaker mention for each of the following physical activities?
textReadable: What advantage does the speaker mention for each of the following physical activities?

body.title: For which TWO reasons does the speaker say people give up going to the gym?
Which TWO pieces of advice does the speaker give for setting goals?

test_text.title: Using natural dyes to colour fabrics

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a textile design student called Jim discussing his project on using natural dyes for coloring fabrics with his tutor.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24. Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, Jim.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You wanted to see me about your textile design project.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I've been looking at how a range of natural dyes can be used to color fabrics like cotton and wool.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Why did you choose that topic?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, I got a lot of useful ideas from the museum.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: You know, at that exhibition of textiles, but I've always been interested in anything to do with color.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Years ago, I went to a carpet shop with my parents when we were on holiday in Turkey, and I remember all the amazing colors.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: They might not all have been natural dyes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Maybe not, but for the project, I decided to follow it up.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And I found a great book about a botanic garden in California that specializes in plants used for dyes.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So in your project, you had to include a practical investigation.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, at first I couldn't decide on my variables.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I was going to just look at one type of fiber for example, like cotton, and see how different types of dyes affected it.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Then I decided to include others as well, so I looked at cotton and wool and nylon.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: With just one type of dye?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Various types, including some that weren't natural for comparison.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So, I did the experiments last week.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I used some ready made natural dyes.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I found a website which supplied them.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: They came in just a few days, but I also made some of my own.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That must have taken quite a bit of time.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I thought it would just be a matter of a teaspoon or so of dye, and actually that wasn't the case at all.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Like I was using one vegetable, beetroot for a red dye.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And I had to chop up a whole pile of it, so it all took longer than I'd expected.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: One possibility is to use food colorings.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I did use one, that was a yellow dye, an artificial one.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Tartrazine?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, I used it on cotton first.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It came out a great color, but when I rinsed the material, the color just washed away.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I'd been going to try it out on nylon, but I abandoned that idea.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Were you worried about health issues?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I thought if it's a legal food coloring, it must be safe.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Well, it can occasionally cause allergic reactions, I believe.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, what natural dyes did you look at?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, one was turmeric.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: The color's great, it's a really strong yellow, it's generally used in dishes like curry.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It's meant to be quite good for your health when eaten, but you might find it's not permanent when it's used as a dye, a few washes and it's gone.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I used beetroot as a dye for wool.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: When I chop up beetroot to eat, I always end up with bright red hands, but the wool ended up just a sort of watery cream shade, disappointing.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: There's a natural dye called Tyrian purple, have you heard of that?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It comes from a shellfish, and it was worn in ancient times, but only by important people, as it was so rare.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I didn't use it.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It fell out of use centuries ago, though one researcher managed to get hold of some recently. But that shade of purple can be produced by chemical dyes nowadays.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Did you use any black dyes?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Logwood.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That was quite complicated.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I had to prepare the fabric so the dye would take.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I hope you were careful to wear gloves.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I know the danger with that dye.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Good, it can be extremely dangerous if it's ingested.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Now presumably you had a look at an insect based dye like cochineal for example.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I didn't actually make that.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I didn't have time to start crushing up insects to get the red color.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And anyway they're not available here, but I managed to get the dye quite easily from a website, but it cost a fortune.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I can see why it's generally just used in cooking and in small quantities.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Yes, it's very effective, but that's precisely why it's not used as a dye.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I also read about using metal oxide.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Apparently you can allow iron to rust while it's in contact with the fabric.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And that colors it.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Yes, that works well for dyeing cotton, but you have to be careful as the metal can actually affect the fabric, and so you can't expect to get a lot of wear out of fabrics treated in this way.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And the colors are quite subtle, not everyone likes them.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Anyway, it looks as if you've done a lot of work.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What first inspired Jim to choose this project?
options: a book about a botanic garden

items.title: Jim eventually decided to do a practical investigation which involved
options: using a range of dyes with different fibres.
applying different dyes to one type of fibre.
testing one dye and a range of fibres.

items.title: When doing his experiments, Jim was surprised by
options: how much natural material was needed to make the dye.
the fact that dyes were widely available on the internet.
the time that he had to leave the fabric in the dye.

items.title: What problem did Jim have with using tartrazine as a fabric dye?
options: It caused a slight allergic reaction.
It was not a permanent dye on cotton.
It was ineffective when used on nylon.

body.title: Project on using natural dyes to colour fabrics

text: What problem is identified with each of the following natural dyes?
textReadable: What problem is identified with each of the following natural dyes?

list: It is unsuitable for some fabrics.
body.title: Natural dyes

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a biology lecture about an animal called the sleepy lizard, that is common in parts of Australia.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Last week we started looking at reptiles, including crocodiles and snakes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, I'd like us to have a look at another reptile, the lizard, and in particular at some studies that have been done on a particular type of lizard, whose Latin name is Tiliqua rugosa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is commonly known as the sleepy lizard, because it's quite slow in its movements and spends quite a lot of its time dozing under rocks or lying in the sun.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'll start with a general description.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sleepy lizards live in Western and South Australia, where they're quite common.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike European lizards, which are mostly small, green and fast-moving,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: sleepy lizards are brown, but what's particularly distinctive about them is the color of their tongue, which is dark blue, in contrast with the lining of their mouth which is bright pink.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they're much bigger than most European lizards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They have quite a varied diet, including insects and even small animals, but they mostly eat plants of varying kinds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Even though they're quite large and powerful, with strong jaws that can crush beetles and snail shells, they still have quite a few predators.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Large birds like cassowaries were one of the main ones in the past.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But nowadays, they're more likely to be caught and killed by snakes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Actually, another threat to their survival isn't a predator at all, but is man made.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Quite a large number of sleepy lizards are killed by cars when they're trying to cross highways.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One study carried out by Michael Freake at Flinders University investigated the methods of navigation of these lizards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though they move slowly, they can travel quite long distances.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And he found that even if they were taken some distance away from their home territory, they could usually find their way back home as long as they could see the sky.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They didn't need any other landmarks on the ground.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Observations of these lizards in the wild have also revealed that their mating habits are quite unusual.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike most animals, it seems that they're relatively monogamous, returning to the same partner year after year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the male and female also stay together for a long time, both before and after the birth of their young.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's quite interesting to think about the possible reasons for this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It could be that it's to do with protecting their young.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'd expect them to have a much better chance of survival.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If they have both parents around, but in fact, observers have noted that once the babies have hatched out of their eggs, they have hardly any contact with their parents.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So there's not really any evidence to support that idea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another suggestion's based on the observation that male lizards in monogamous relationships tend to be bigger and stronger than other males.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So maybe the male lizards stay around, so they can give the female lizards protection from other males.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But again, we're not really sure.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finally, I'd like to mention another study that involved collecting data by tracking the lizards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I was actually involved in this myself, so we caught some lizards in the wild.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we developed a tiny GPS system that would allow us to track them, and we fixed this onto their tails.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then we set the lizards free again, and we were able to track them for 12 days and gather data, not just about their location, but even about how many steps they took during this period.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One surprising thing we discovered from this is that there were far fewer meetings between lizards than we expected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It seems that they were actually trying to avoid one another.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So why would that be?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, again, we have no clear evidence, but one hypothesis is that male lizards can cause quite serious injuries to one another.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So maybe this avoidance is a way of preventing this, of self preservation if you like.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we need to collect a lot more data before we can be sure of any of this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.

utterances.text: Large birds like cassowaries were one of the main ones in the past.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But nowadays, they're more likely to be caught and killed by snakes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Actually, another threat to their survival isn't a predator at all, but is man made.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Quite a large number of sleepy lizards are killed by cars when they're trying to cross highways.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One study carried out by Michael Freake at Flinders University investigated the methods of navigation of these lizards.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Though they move slowly, they can travel quite long distances.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And he found that even if they were taken some distance away from their home territory, they could usually find their way back home as long as they could see the sky.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They didn't need any other landmarks on the ground.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Observations of these lizards in the wild have also revealed that their mating habits are quite unusual.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unlike most animals, it seems that they're relatively monogamous, returning to the same partner year after year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the male and female also stay together for a long time, both before and after the birth of their young.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's quite interesting to think about the possible reasons for this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It could be that it's to do with protecting their young.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'd expect them to have a much better chance of survival.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If they have both parents around, but in fact, observers have noted that once the babies have hatched out of their eggs, they have hardly any contact with their parents.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So there's not really any evidence to support that idea.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another suggestion's based on the observation that male lizards in monogamous relationships tend to be bigger and stronger than other males.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So maybe the male lizards stay around, so they can give the female lizards protection from other males.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But again, we're not really sure.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Finally, I'd like to mention another study that involved collecting data by tracking the lizards.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I was actually involved in this myself, so we caught some lizards in the wild.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And we developed a tiny GPS system that would allow us to track them, and we fixed this onto their tails.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then we set the lizards free again, and we were able to track them for 12 days and gather data, not just about their location, but even about how many steps they took during this period.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One surprising thing we discovered from this is that there were far fewer meetings between lizards than we expected.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It seems that they were actually trying to avoid one another.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So why would that be?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, again, we have no clear evidence, but one hypothesis is that male lizards can cause quite serious injuries to one another.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So maybe this avoidance is a way of preventing this, of self preservation if you like.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But we need to collect a lot more data before we can be sure of any of this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
